TL;DR: The paper introduces OmniForcing, a framework that converts offline dual-stream bidirectional diffusion models into efficient streaming autoregressive generators, achieving high-quality audio-visual generation with lower latency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current advanced audio-visual diffusion models deliver excellent generation quality but cannot perform optimally for real-time applications due to slow bidirectional attention mechanisms.

Method: OmniForcing addresses training instability by resolving temporal asymmetry through Asymmetric Block-Causal Alignment, mitigating audio sparsity issues with an Audio Sink Token mechanism, and minimizing exposure bias via Joint Self-Forcing Distillation. Additionally, it employs rolling KV-cache inference for efficient synchronous multi-modal output.

Result: OmniForcing achieves streaming generation at ~25 FPS on a single GPU, maintaining synchronization and quality comparable to the bidirectional teacher model.

Conclusion: The framework effectively bridges the gap between real-time performance and high-quality output for audio-visual generation models, paving the way for better streaming applications.

Abstract: Recent joint audio-visual diffusion models achieve remarkable generation quality but suffer from high latency due to their bidirectional attention dependencies, hindering real-time applications. We propose OmniForcing, the first framework to distill an offline, dual-stream bidirectional diffusion model into a high-fidelity streaming autoregressive generator. However, naively applying causal distillation to such dual-stream architectures triggers severe training instability, due to the extreme temporal asymmetry between modalities and the resulting token sparsity. We address the inherent information density gap by introducing an Asymmetric Block-Causal Alignment with a zero-truncation Global Prefix that prevents multi-modal synchronization drift. The gradient explosion caused by extreme audio token sparsity during the causal shift is further resolved through an Audio Sink Token mechanism equipped with an Identity RoPE constraint. Finally, a Joint Self-Forcing Distillation paradigm enables the model to dynamically self-correct cumulative cross-modal errors from exposure bias during long rollouts. Empowered by a modality-independent rolling KV-cache inference scheme, OmniForcing achieves state-of-the-art streaming generation at $\sim$25 FPS on a single GPU, maintaining multi-modal synchronization and visual quality on par with the bidirectional teacher.\textbf{Project Page:} \href{https://omniforcing.com}{https://omniforcing.com}

</details>


<div id='cond-mat.mtrl-sci'></div>

# cond-mat.mtrl-sci [[Back]](#toc)

### [451] [From Phase Prediction to Phase Design: A ReAct Agent Framework for High-Entropy Alloy Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11068)
*Iman Peivaste,Salim Belouettar*

Main category: cond-mat.mtrl-sci

TL;DR: This paper introduces a ReAct (Reasoning + Acting) large language model (LLM) agent to discover and optimize high-entropy alloy (HEA) compositions efficiently.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The challenge addressed in this paper is the high-dimensional inverse design of HEA compositions for target crystal phases, which cannot be effectively handled by traditional trial-and-error methods or standard forward-only machine learning models.

Method: A ReAct (Reasoning + Acting) LLM agent is implemented, combining predictive modeling through a calibrated XGBoost trained on 4,753 experimental records and iterative proposal refinement.

Result: The agent achieves a 94.66% accuracy (F1 macro=0.896), demonstrating significant improvement over Bayesian optimization and random search across several rediscovery and exploration metrics.

Conclusion: LLM-driven agentic reasoning provides a promising and more efficient approach to HEA design, balancing literature-based rediscovery with novel exploration, thus complementing gradient-free methods for inverse problem-solving in materials science.

Abstract: Discovering high-entropy alloy (HEA) compositions that reliably form a target crystal phase is a high-dimensional inverse design problem that conventional trial-and-error experimentation and forward-only machine learning models cannot efficiently solve. Here we present a ReAct (Reasoning + Acting) LLM agent that autonomously proposes, validates, and iteratively refines HEA compositions by querying a calibrated XGBoost surrogate trained on 4,753 experimental records across four phases (FCC, BCC, BCC+FCC, BCC+IM), achieving 94.66\% accuracy (F1 macro = 0.896). Against Bayesian optimisation (BO) and random search baselines, the full-prompt agent achieves descriptor-space rediscovery rates of 38\%, 18\%, and 38\% for FCC, BCC, and BCC+FCC (Mann--Whitney $p \leq 0.039$), with proposals lying 2.4--22.8$\times$ closer to the experimental phase manifold than random search. An ablation reveals that domain priors shift the agent from landmark-alloy recall toward compositionally diverse exploration -- an uninformed agent scores higher rediscovery by concentrating on literature-dense families, while the full-prompt agent explores underrepresented space (unique ratio 1.0 vs.\ 0.39 for BCC+FCC). These regimes represent distinct criteria: proximity to known literature versus genuine discovery. Spearman analysis confirms agent reasoning is statistically aligned with empirical phase distributions ($ρ= 0.736$, $p = 0.004$ for BCC). This work establishes LLM-guided agentic reasoning as a principled, transparent, and manifold-aware complement to gradient-free optimisation for inverse alloy design.

</details>


### [452] [Proof-Carrying Materials: Falsifiable Safety Certificates for Machine-Learned Interatomic Potentials](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12183)
*Abhinaba Basu,Pavan Chakraborty*

Main category: cond-mat.mtrl-sci

TL;DR: Machine-learned interatomic potentials (MLIPs) often fail in identifying stable materials accurately. Proof-Carrying Materials (PCM) provides a systematic auditing framework with formal reliability guarantees, improving discovery yield in materials screenings significantly.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current MLIPs are utilized in materials screening but lack formal reliability and accuracy guarantees, leading to missed opportunities in identifying DFT-stable materials.

Method: PCM employs three stages: adversarial falsification across compositional space, refinement with bootstrap envelope confidence intervals, and formal certification using Lean 4.

Result: MLIPs commonly exhibit blind spots, missing out on stable materials during screenings. PCM improves reliability, predicts MLIP failures (high AUC-ROC scores), and enhances discovery yields in case studies such as thermoelectric materials.

Conclusion: PCM offers a robust, reliable solution to augment materials discovery and MLIP effectiveness, closing gaps in stability assessments and enhancing scientific outcomes.

Abstract: Machine-learned interatomic potentials (MLIPs) are deployed for high-throughput materials screening without formal reliability guarantees. We show that a single MLIP used as a stability filter misses 93% of density functional theory (DFT)-stable materials (recall 0.07) on a 25,000-material benchmark. Proof-Carrying Materials (PCM) closes this gap through three stages: adversarial falsification across compositional space, bootstrap envelope refinement with 95% confidence intervals, and Lean 4 formal certification. Auditing CHGNet, TensorNet and MACE reveals architecture-specific blind spots with near-zero pairwise error correlations (r <= 0.13; n = 5,000), confirmed by independent Quantum ESPRESSO validation (20/20 converged; median DFT/CHGNet force ratio 12x). A risk model trained on PCM-discovered features predicts failures on unseen materials (AUC-ROC = 0.938 +/- 0.004) and transfers across architectures (cross-MLIP AUC-ROC ~ 0.70; feature importance r = 0.877). In a thermoelectric screening case study, PCM-audited protocols discover 62 additional stable materials missed by single-MLIP screening - a 25% improvement in discovery yield.

</details>


<div id='math.GR'></div>

# math.GR [[Back]](#toc)

### [453] [A Diffeomorphism Groupoid and Algebroid Framework for Discontinuous Image Registration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11806)
*Lili Bao,Bin Xiao,Shihui Ying,Stefan Sommer*

Main category: math.GR

TL;DR: The paper proposes a mathematical framework for image registration incorporating discontinuous sliding motion by extending the LDDMM approach to a novel discontinuous diffeomorphism framework.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional LDDMM registration struggles with discontinuous sliding motion due to its reliance on continuous and smooth velocity fields.

Method: The authors introduce a discontinuous diffeomorphism Lie groupoid framework, conduct a mathematical analysis of its structures, and derive Euler-Arnold equations for optimal flows.

Result: The framework successfully allows for discontinuities along sliding boundaries while maintaining diffeomorphisms within regions. Numerical tests validate its efficiency.

Conclusion: This novel framework expands the applicability of image registration techniques to handle problems involving discontinuous motion.

Abstract: In this paper, we propose a novel mathematical framework for piecewise diffeomorphic image registration that involves discontinuous sliding motion using a diffeomorphism groupoid and algebroid approach. The traditional Large Deformation Diffeomorphic Metric Mapping (LDDMM) registration method builds on Lie groups, which assume continuity and smoothness in velocity fields, limiting its applicability in handling discontinuous sliding motion. To overcome this limitation, we extend the diffeomorphism Lie groups to a framework of discontinuous diffeomorphism Lie groupoids, allowing for discontinuities along sliding boundaries while maintaining diffeomorphism within homogeneous regions. We provide a rigorous analysis of the associated mathematical structures, including Lie algebroids and their duals, and derive specific Euler-Arnold equations to govern optimal flows for discontinuous deformations. Some numerical tests are performed to validate the efficiency of the proposed approach.

</details>


<div id='math.OC'></div>

# math.OC [[Back]](#toc)

### [454] [Simultaneous estimation of multiple discrete unimodal distributions under stochastic order constraints](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11532)
*Yasuhiro Yoshida,Noriyoshi Sukegawa,Jiro Iwanaga*

Main category: math.OC

TL;DR: The paper presents a method to estimate multiple discrete unimodal distributions with stochastic order constraints, offering improvements in Jensen-Shannon divergence for small sample sizes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study is motivated by the need to analyze search behavior on real-world platforms while incorporating precedence relations among distributions.

Method: The authors formulate the estimation task as a mixed-integer convex quadratic optimization problem, leveraging stochastic order constraints.

Result: The method reduces Jensen-Shannon divergence by an average of 2.2% (up to 6.3%) for small sample sizes, with performance comparable to existing approaches when ample data is available.

Conclusion: The proposed optimization approach is effective in improving distribution estimation under constraints, especially with limited data.

Abstract: We study the problem of estimating multiple discrete unimodal distributions, motivated by search behavior analysis on a real-world platform. To incorporate prior knowledge of precedence relations among distributions, we impose stochastic order constraints and formulate the estimation task as a mixed-integer convex quadratic optimization problem. Experiments on both synthetic and real datasets show that the proposed method reduces the Jensen-Shannon divergence by 2.2% on average (up to 6.3%) when the sample size is small, while performing comparably to existing methods when sufficient data are available.

</details>


<div id='cs.MA'></div>

# cs.MA [[Back]](#toc)

### [455] [Enhancing Value Alignment of LLMs with Multi-agent system and Combinatorial Fusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11126)
*Yuanhong Wu,Djallel Bouneffouf,D. Frank Hsu*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: This paper introduces VAS-CFA, a framework for better aligning large language models with human values using multi-agent fusion, outperforming existing methods like RLHF.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitations of existing alignment methods, such as RLHF, which rely on a single evaluator or narrowly defined reward signals, restricting ethical pluralism.

Method: VAS-CFA employs multiple moral agents tuned to different normative perspectives and combines their outputs using Combinatorial Fusion Analysis with rank- and score-based aggregation.

Result: Empirical tests show VAS-CFA surpasses single-agent baselines and prior methods in achieving stronger alignment outcomes across standard metrics.

Conclusion: Multi-agent fusion leveraging cognitive diversity offers a promising solution for improving ethical and trustworthy value alignment in large language models.

Abstract: Aligning large language models (LLMs) with human values is a central challenge for ensuring trustworthy and safe deployment. While existing methods such as Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) and its variants have improved alignment, they often rely on a single evaluator or narrowly defined reward signals, limiting their ability to capture ethical pluralism. In this work, we propose the Value Alignment System using Combinatorial Fusion Analysis (VAS-CFA), a framework that operationalizes multi-agent fusion alignment. It instantiates multiple moral agents, each fine-tuned to represent a distinct normative perspective, and fuses their outputs using CFA with both rank- and score-based aggregation. This design leverages cognitive diversity, between agents, to mitigate conflicts and redundancies across multiple agents, producing responses that better reflect human values. Empirical evaluation demonstrates that VAS-CFA outperforms both single agent baselines and prior aggregation approaches on standard metrics, showing that multi-agent fusion provides a robust and effective mechanism for advancing value alignment in LLMs.

</details>


### [456] [How Intelligence Emerges: A Minimal Theory of Dynamic Adaptive Coordination](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11560)
*Stefano Grassi*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: This paper proposes a dynamic theory for adaptive coordination in multi-agent systems, emphasizing its structural properties rather than solving centralized optimization problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To redefine how coordination in multi-agent systems is analyzed, moving past traditional equilibrium optimization or agent-based learning towards a holistic approach involving agents, incentives, and environment.

Method: A framework models the system as a recursively closed feedback architecture involving adaptive agents, incentive fields, and persistent environmental memory.

Result: The paper proves three structural results: viability of the closed-loop system under dissipativity, non-reducibility to static global objectives with environmental memory, and history sensitivity induced by persistent environmental state.

Conclusion: Intelligent coordination dynamics can emerge from adaptive interaction within a persistent environment under specific structural conditions, without relying on welfare maximization or centralized design.

Abstract: This paper develops a dynamical theory of adaptive coordination in multi-agent systems. Rather than analyzing coordination through equilibrium optimization or agent-centric learning alone, the framework models agents, incentives, and environment as a recursively closed feedback architecture. A persistent environment stores accumulated coordination signals, a distributed incentive field transmits those signals locally, and adaptive agents update in response. Coordination is thus treated as a structural property of coupled dynamics rather than as the solution to a centralized objective.
  The paper establishes three structural results. First, under dissipativity assumptions, the induced closed-loop system admits a bounded forward-invariant region, ensuring viability without requiring global optimality. Second, when incentive signals depend non-trivially on persistent environmental memory, the resulting dynamics generically cannot be reduced to a static global objective defined solely over the agent state space. Third, persistent environmental state induces history sensitivity unless the system is globally contracting.
  A minimal linear specification illustrates how coupling, persistence, and dissipation govern local stability and oscillatory regimes through spectral conditions on the Jacobian. The results establish structural conditions under which intelligent coordination dynamics emerge from incentive-mediated adaptive interaction within a persistent environment, without presuming welfare maximization, rational expectations, or centralized design.

</details>


### [457] [Hybrid Human-Agent Social Dilemmas in Energy Markets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11834)
*Isuri Perera,Frits de Nijs,Julian Garcia*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: This paper investigates the emergence of cooperative behavior in hybrid populations where humans use agents in energy load management. It examines partial adoption and strategic implications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand and promote cooperation in hybrid systems involving humans and autonomous agents, specifically in the context of managing energy demand.

Method: The authors use globally observable signals implemented by artificial agents, evolutionary dynamics, and reinforcement learning experiments to analyze behavior and coordination in hybrid populations.

Result: Artificial agents were shown to promote cooperative behavior, making collective outcomes more efficient even in mixed populations of adopters and non-adopters.

Conclusion: Artificial agents can improve social outcomes, but adoption scenarios must be carefully considered, as non-adopters could disproportionately benefit from cooperative dynamics enabled by adopters.

Abstract: In hybrid populations where humans delegate strategic decision-making to autonomous agents, understanding when and how cooperative behaviors can emerge remains a key challenge. We study this problem in the context of energy load management: consumer agents schedule their appliance use under demand-dependent pricing. This structure can create a social dilemma where everybody would benefit from coordination, but in equilibrium agents often choose to incur the congestion costs that cooperative turn-taking would avoid. To address the problem of coordination, we introduce artificial agents that use globally observable signals to increase coordination. Using evolutionary dynamics, and reinforcement learning experiments, we show that artificial agents can shift the learning dynamics to favour coordination outcomes. An often neglected problem is partial adoption: what happens when the technology of artificial agents is in the early adoption stages? We analyze mixed populations of adopters and non-adopters, demonstrating that unilateral entry is feasible: adopters are not structurally penalized, and partial adoption can still improve aggregate outcomes. However, in some parameter regimes, non-adopters may benefit disproportionately from the cooperation induced by adopters. This asymmetry, while not precluding beneficial entry, warrants consideration in deployment, and highlights strategic issues around the adoption of AI technology in multiagent settings.

</details>


<div id='cs.NI'></div>

# cs.NI [[Back]](#toc)

### [458] [Agentic AI for Embodied-enhanced Beam Prediction in Low-Altitude Economy Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11392)
*Min Hao,Zhizhuo Li,Zirui Zhang,Maoqiang Wu,Han Zhang,Rong Yu*

Main category: cs.NI

TL;DR: The paper explores enhancing UAV mmWave communications by introducing multi-agent collaborative reasoning and hybrid beam prediction models to achieve high prediction accuracy and robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address challenges of high propagation loss and beam directivity in mmWave communications for mobile UAV scenarios.

Method: A multi-agent collaborative reasoning architecture and hybrid beam prediction model using multitask decomposition, temporal modeling, visual encoding, and multimodal fusion are implemented.

Result: Proposed system achieves high prediction accuracy (max top-1 accuracy of 96.57%) and robustness in simulations with real UAV communication datasets.

Conclusion: Integrating multi-agent reasoning with hybrid prediction models improves UAV-to-ground mmWave communication, solving challenges in real-time decision-making.

Abstract: Millimeter-wave or terahertz communications can meet demands of low-altitude economy networks for high-throughput sensing and real-time decision making. However, high-frequency characteristics of wireless channels result in severe propagation loss and strong beam directivity, which make beam prediction challenging in highly mobile uncrewed aerial vehicles (UAV) scenarios. In this paper, we employ agentic AI to enable the transformation of mmWave base stations toward embodied intelligence. We innovatively design a multi-agent collaborative reasoning architecture for UAV-to-ground mmWave communications and propose a hybrid beam prediction model system based on bimodal data. The multi-agent architecture is designed to overcome the limited context window and weak controllability of large language model (LLM)-based reasoning by decomposing beam prediction into task analysis, solution planning, and completeness assessment. To align with the agentic reasoning process, a hybrid beam prediction model system is developed to process multimodal UAV data, including numeric mobility information and visual observations. The proposed hybrid model system integrates Mamba-based temporal modelling, convolutional visual encoding, and cross-attention-based multimodal fusion, and dynamically switches data-flow strategies under multi-agent guidance. Extensive simulations on a real UAV mmWave communication dataset demonstrate that proposed architecture and system achieve high prediction accuracy and robustness under diverse data conditions, with maximum top-1 accuracy reaching 96.57%.

</details>


### [459] [Efficient Cross-View Localization in 6G Space-Air-Ground Integrated Network](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11398)
*Min Hao,Yanbing Xu,Maoqiang Wu,Jinglin Huang,Chen Shang,Jiacheng Wang,Ruichen Zhang,Jiawen Kang,Dusit Niyato,Zhu Han,Wei Ni*

Main category: cs.NI

TL;DR: This paper explores how integrating cross-view localization (CVL) with 6G SAGIN enhances performance in latency, energy consumption, and privacy, proposing a split-inference framework with joint optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper is motivated by the need to improve localization reliability and efficiency by integrating advanced CVL approaches with future 6G space-air-ground integrated networks.

Method: The authors first review CVL and SAGIN. Then, they propose a split-inference framework, utilizing distributed resources in 6G SAGIN architecture. They also perform joint optimization of communication, computation, and confidentiality.

Result: The proposed framework demonstrates improved performance in localization accuracy and processing speed, validated through experiments that solve the associated optimization problems.

Conclusion: Integrating CVL with 6G SAGIN significantly enhances localization performance, providing both practical benefits and a new direction for future research.

Abstract: Recently, visual localization has become an important supplement to improve localization reliability, and cross-view approaches can greatly enhance coverage and adaptability. Meanwhile, future 6G will enable a globally covered mobile communication system, with a space-air-ground integrated network (SAGIN) serving as key supporting architecture. Inspired by this, we explore an integration of cross-view localization (CVL) with 6G SAGIN, thereby enhancing its performance in latency, energy consumption, and privacy protection. First, we provide a comprehensive review of CVL and SAGIN, highlighting their capabilities, integration opportunities, and potential applications. Benefiting from the fast and extensive image collection and transmission capabilities of the 6G SAGIN architecture, CVL achieves higher localization accuracy and faster processing speed. Then, we propose a split-inference framework for implementing CVL, which fully leverages the distributed communication and computing resources of the 6G SAGIN architecture. Subsequently, we conduct joint optimization of communication, computation, and confidentiality within the proposed split-inference framework, aiming to provide a paradigm and a direction for making CVL efficient. Experimental results validate the effectiveness of the proposed framework and provide solutions to the optimization problem. Finally, we discuss potential research directions for 6G SAGIN-enabled CVL.

</details>


<div id='hep-th'></div>

# hep-th [[Back]](#toc)

### [460] [Learning to Unscramble: Simplifying Symbolic Expressions via Self-Supervised Oracle Trajectories](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11164)
*David Shih*

Main category: hep-th

TL;DR: The paper introduces a self-supervised, transformer-based machine learning model for simplifying complex mathematical expressions, achieving 100% success rates on challenging problems in high-energy physics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods struggle to efficiently simplify complex mathematical expressions, particularly in fields like high-energy physics. There is a need for advanced models to outperform traditional and reinforcement learning approaches in simplification tasks.

Method: The authors use a self-supervised learning framework that generates training data from scrambled expressions using oracle trajectories. They train a permutation-equivariant transformer-based policy network to predict simplification steps using these trajectories.

Result: The trained model demonstrates near-perfect solve rates, outperforming previous reinforcement learning and regression-based methods. It achieves 100% simplification success on complex 5-point gluon tree-level amplitudes in Yang-Mills theory.

Conclusion: The proposed approach is highly effective at simplifying mathematical expressions, particularly in high-energy physics. This opens up new potential for applying self-supervised learning to other symbolic simplification problems.

Abstract: We present a new self-supervised machine learning approach for symbolic simplification of complex mathematical expressions. Training data is generated by scrambling simple expressions and recording the inverse operations, creating oracle trajectories that provide both goal states and explicit paths to reach them. A permutation-equivariant, transformer-based policy network is then trained on this data step-wise to predict the oracle action given the input expression. We demonstrate this approach on two problems in high-energy physics: dilogarithm reduction and spinor-helicity scattering amplitude simplification. In both cases, our trained policy network achieves near perfect solve rates across a wide range of difficulty levels, substantially outperforming prior approaches based on reinforcement learning and end-to-end regression. When combined with contrastive grouping and beam search, our model achieves a 100\% full simplification rate on a representative selection of 5-point gluon tree-level amplitudes in Yang-Mills theory, including expressions with over 200 initial terms.

</details>


<div id='q-bio.GN'></div>

# q-bio.GN [[Back]](#toc)

### [461] [A Standardized Framework For Evaluating Gene Expression Generative Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11244)
*Andrea Rubbi,Andrea Giuseppe Di Francesco,Mohammad Lotfollahi,Pietro Liò*

Main category: q-bio.GN

TL;DR: GGE (Generated Genetic Expression Evaluator) provides a standardised evaluation framework for single-cell gene expression generative models by introducing biologically grounded metrics and tools for consistency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluation methods for single-cell generative models suffer from inconsistencies in implementation, hyperparameter selection, and lack of biologically meaningful metrics.

Method: The paper introduces GGE, an open-source Python framework, offering biologically-focused metrics like DEG-based analysis and perturbation-effect correlation, alongside tools for controlled reporting and benchmarking.

Result: The study highlights substantial variations in metric values due to differing implementations and demonstrates how GGE allows for standardized, reproducible evaluations.

Conclusion: GGE facilitates fair comparisons among generative approaches, advancing progress in key areas including perturbation response prediction, cellular identity modeling, and counterfactual inference.

Abstract: The rapid development of generative models for single-cell gene expression data has created an urgent need for standardised evaluation frameworks. Current evaluation practices suffer from inconsistent metric implementations, incomparable hyperparameter choices, and a lack of biologically-grounded metrics. We present Generated Genetic Expression Evaluator (GGE), an open-source Python framework that addresses these challenges by providing a comprehensive suite of distributional metrics with explicit computation space options and biologically-motivated evaluation through differentially expressed gene (DEG)-focused analysis and perturbation-effect correlation, enabling standardized reporting and reproducible benchmarking. Through extensive analysis of the single-cell generative modeling literature, we identify that no standardized evaluation protocol exists. Methods report incomparable metrics computed in different spaces with different hyperparameters. We demonstrate that metric values vary substantially depending on implementation choices, highlighting the critical need for standardization. GGE enables fair comparison across generative approaches and accelerates progress in perturbation response prediction, cellular identity modeling, and counterfactual inference.

</details>


### [462] [ELISA: An Interpretable Hybrid Generative AI Agent for Expression-Grounded Discovery in Single-Cell Genomics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11872)
*Omar Coser*

Main category: q-bio.GN

TL;DR: ELISA is a new framework combining scGPT embeddings and BioBERT-based semantic retrieval for single-cell RNA sequencing data analysis. It integrates interactive modules, outperforming CellWhisperer in diverse benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a critical bottleneck in translating single-cell RNA sequencing data into biological hypotheses due to AI challenges with transcriptomic representations.

Method: ELISA combines scGPT embeddings, BioBERT semantic search, and LLM-mediated interpretation. It leverages signature routing and analytical modules for analyzing embedded data.

Result: ELISA outperformed CellWhisperer in cell type retrieval and gene-signature queries across diverse benchmarks. It replicated biological findings with high accuracy and generated hypotheses using grounded reasoning.

Conclusion: ELISA bridges the gap between transcriptomic data exploration and biological discovery by offering an interpretable and high-performing analysis framework.

Abstract: Translating single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) data into mechanistic biological hypotheses remains a critical bottleneck, as agentic AI systems lack direct access to transcriptomic representations while expression foundation models remain opaque to natural language. Here we introduce ELISA (Embedding-Linked Interactive Single-cell Agent), an interpretable framework that unifies scGPT expression embeddings with BioBERT-based semantic retrieval and LLM-mediated interpretation for interactive single-cell discovery. An automatic query classifier routes inputs to gene marker scoring, semantic matching, or reciprocal rank fusion pipelines depending on whether the query is a gene signature, natural language concept, or mixture of both. Integrated analytical modules perform pathway activity scoringacross 60+ gene sets, ligand--receptor interaction prediction using 280+ curated pairs, condition-aware comparative analysis, and cell-type proportion estimation all operating directly on embedded data without access to the original count matrix. Benchmarked across six diverse scRNA-seq datasets spanning inflammatory lung disease, pediatric and adult cancers, organoid models, healthy tissue, and neurodevelopment, ELISA significantly outperforms CellWhisperer in cell type retrieval (combined permutation test, $p < 0.001$), with particularly large gains on gene-signature queries (Cohen's $d = 5.98$ for MRR). ELISA replicates published biological findings (mean composite score 0.90) with near-perfect pathway alignment and theme coverage (0.98 each), and generates candidate hypotheses through grounded LLM reasoning, bridging the gap between transcriptomic data exploration and biological discovery. Code available at: https://github.com/omaruno/ELISA-An-AI-Agent-for-Expression-Grounded-Discovery-in-Single-Cell-Genomics.git (If you use ELISA in your research, please cite this work).

</details>


<div id='q-bio.QM'></div>

# q-bio.QM [[Back]](#toc)

### [463] [Ill-Conditioning in Dictionary-Based Dynamic-Equation Learning: A Systems Biology Case Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11330)
*Yuxiang Feng,Niall M Mangan,Manu Jayadharan*

Main category: q-bio.QM

TL;DR: This paper addresses numerical ill-conditioning caused by multicollinearity in sparse regression for biological system identification, evaluating different basis functions and sampling strategies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve the reliability of data-driven methods for identifying governing equations of biological systems, especially under challenging sampling and noise conditions.

Method: The authors systematically analyze numerical ill-conditioning using benchmark biological models, comparing monomial and orthogonal polynomial bases, and investigating data sampling strategies.

Result: Multicollinearity was observed to occur with as few as two terms, and orthogonal polynomial bases failed under mismatched data distributions. However, sampling aligned with appropriate distributions improved conditioning and recovery accuracy.

Conclusion: Proper data sampling aligned with orthogonal basis functions enhances model recovery accuracy and mitigates issues with numerical instability in sparse biological system identification.

Abstract: Data-driven discovery of governing equations from time-series data provides a powerful framework for understanding complex biological systems. Library-based approaches that use sparse regression over candidate functions have shown considerable promise, but they face a critical challenge when candidate functions become strongly correlated: numerical ill-conditioning. Poor or restricted sampling, together with particular choices of candidate libraries, can produce strong multicollinearity and numerical instability. In such cases, measurement noise may lead to widely different recovered models, obscuring the true underlying dynamics and hindering accurate system identification. Although sparse regularization promotes parsimonious solutions and can partially mitigate conditioning issues, strong correlations may persist, regularization may bias the recovered models, and the regression problem may remain highly sensitive to small perturbations in the data. We present a systematic analysis of how ill-conditioning affects sparse identification of biological dynamics using benchmark models from systems biology. We show that combinations involving as few as two or three terms can already exhibit strong multicollinearity and extremely large condition numbers. We further show that orthogonal polynomial bases do not consistently resolve ill-conditioning and can perform worse than monomial libraries when the data distribution deviates from the weight function associated with the orthogonal basis. Finally, we demonstrate that when data are sampled from distributions aligned with the appropriate weight functions corresponding to the orthogonal basis, numerical conditioning improves, and orthogonal polynomial bases can yield improved model recovery accuracy across two baseline models.

</details>


<div id='stat.ME'></div>

# stat.ME [[Back]](#toc)

### [464] [Partition-Based Functional Ridge Regression for High-Dimensional Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11113)
*Shaista Ashraf,Ismail Shah,Farrukh Javed*

Main category: stat.ME

TL;DR: The paper introduces a partition-based ridge regression method for high-dimensional functional models, addressing overfitting, multicollinearity, and interpretability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve interpretability, numerical stability, and address multicollinearity and overfitting issues in functional linear models.

Method: Decomposing the coefficient function into dominant and weaker effects, applying differential ridge penalization, and developing three estimators (FRE, FRFM, FRSM) with established theoretical properties.

Result: Simulation experiments and application to Canadian weather data showed improved predictive performance, reduced variance inflation, and clearer identification of influential effects.

Conclusion: Partition-based ridge regularization is effective for high-dimensional functional regression, offering practical and theoretically sound improvements.

Abstract: This paper proposes a partition-based functional ridge regression framework to address multicollinearity, overfitting, and interpretability in high-dimensional functional linear models. The coefficient function vector \( \boldsymbolβ(s) \) is decomposed into two components, \( \boldsymbolβ_1(s) \) and \( \boldsymbolβ_2(s) \), representing dominant and weaker functional effects. This partition enables differential ridge penalization across functional blocks, so that important signals are preserved while less informative components are more strongly shrunk. The resulting approach improves numerical stability and enhances interpretability without relying on explicit variable selection. We develop three estimators: the Functional Ridge Estimator (FRE), the Functional Ridge Full Model (FRFM), and the Functional Ridge Sub-Model (FRSM). Under standard regularity conditions, we establish consistency and asymptotic normality for all estimators. Simulation results reveal a clear bias--variance trade-off where FRSM performs best in small samples through strong variance reduction, whereas FRFM achieves superior accuracy in moderate to large samples by retaining informative functional structure through adaptive penalization. An empirical application to Canadian weather data further demonstrates improved predictive performance, reduced variance inflation, and clearer identification of influential functional effects. Overall, partition-based ridge regularization provides a practical and theoretically grounded method for high-dimensional functional regression.

</details>


### [465] [Outrigger local polynomial regression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11282)
*Elliot H. Young,Rajen D. Shah,Richard J. Samworth*

Main category: stat.ME

TL;DR: The paper introduces the outrigger local polynomial estimator, improving distributional adaptability in nonparametric regression by utilizing conditional score functions and outrigger techniques.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve the flexibility of local polynomial estimators in dealing with diverse conditional error distributions, beyond the Gaussian assumption.

Method: The outrigger estimator modifies standard local polynomial approaches with conditional score function estimates and data drawn from broader windows to enhance stability.

Result: The outrigger estimator is shown to be at least as good as traditional methods in worst-case scenarios and is minimax optimal up to a factor dependent on smoothness and dimensionality.

Conclusion: The proposed method offers wider applicability without strong structural assumptions and demonstrates theoretical and numerical validation, with implementation available in R.

Abstract: Standard local polynomial estimators of a nonparametric regression function employ a weighted least squares loss function that is tailored to the setting of homoscedastic Gaussian errors. We introduce the outrigger local polynomial estimator, which is designed to achieve distributional adaptivity across different conditional error distributions. It modifies a standard local polynomial estimator by employing an estimate of the conditional score function of the errors and an 'outrigger' that draws on the data in a broader local window to stabilise the influence of the conditional score estimate. Subject to smoothness and moment conditions, and only requiring consistency of the conditional score estimate, we first establish that even under the least favourable settings for the outrigger estimator, the asymptotic ratio of the worst-case local risks of the two estimators is at most $1$, with equality if and only if the conditional error distribution is Gaussian. Moreover, we prove that the outrigger estimator is minimax optimal over Hölder classes up to a multiplicative factor $A_{β,d}$, depending only on the smoothness $β\in (0,\infty)$ of the regression function and the dimension~$d$ of the covariates. When $β\in (0,1]$, we find that $A_{β,d} \leq 1.69$, with $\lim_{β\searrow 0} A_{β,d} = 1$. A further attraction of our proposal is that we do not require structural assumptions such as independence of errors and covariates, or symmetry of the conditional error distribution. Numerical results on simulated and real data validate our theoretical findings; our methodology is implemented in R and available at https://github.com/elliot-young/outrigger.

</details>


### [466] [Data Fusion with Distributional Equivalence Test-then-pool](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11867)
*Linying Yang,Xing Liu,Robin J. Evans*

Main category: stat.ME

TL;DR: The paper introduces a new test-then-pool (TTP) framework using kernel two-sample tests and equivalence testing to improve efficiency in clinical trials by borrowing historical control data while rigorously controlling errors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Randomized controlled trials (RCTs) often face practical constraints limiting the size of control arms, and borrowing data from historical controls offers efficiency gains but risks bias if populations differ. Existing methods lack power or sufficient error control.

Method: The authors utilize kernel two-sample testing via maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) and equivalence testing to pool control arms. They develop partial bootstrap and partial permutation procedures to approximate null distributions for valid inference.

Result: Empirical studies show the proposed method achieves higher power than standard TTP approaches while maintaining nominal Type-I error control, making it effective for leveraging historical data.

Conclusion: The proposed framework improves the reliability and efficiency of clinical trials by mitigating bias and ensuring rigorous error control, making it a valuable method for using historical controls.

Abstract: Randomized controlled trials (RCTs) are the gold standard for causal inference, yet practical constraints often limit the size of the concurrent control arm. Borrowing control data from previous trials offers a potential efficiency gain, but naive borrowing can induce bias when historical and current populations differ. Existing test-then-pool (TTP) procedures address this concern by testing for equality of control outcomes between historical and concurrent trials before borrowing; however, standard implementations may suffer from reduced power or inadequate control of the Type-I error rate.
  We develop a new TTP framework that fuses control arms while rigorously controlling the Type-I error rate of the final treatment effect test. Our method employs kernel two-sample testing via maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) to capture distributional differences, and equivalence testing to avoid introducing uncontrolled bias, providing a more flexible and informative criterion for pooling. To ensure valid inference, we introduce partial bootstrap and partial permutation procedures for approximating null distributions in the presence of heterogeneous controls. We further establish the overall validity and consistency. We provide empirical studies demonstrating that the proposed approach achieves higher power than standard TTP methods while maintaining nominal error control, highlighting its value as a principled tool for leveraging historical controls in modern clinical trials.

</details>


<div id='quant-ph'></div>

# quant-ph [[Back]](#toc)

### [467] [Quantum mechanical framework for quantization-based optimization: from Gradient flow to Schroedinger equation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11536)
*Jinwuk Seok,Changsik Cho*

Main category: quant-ph

TL;DR: The paper presents a quantum mechanical framework for quantization-based optimization, leveraging quantum tunneling, thermodynamic interpretations, and unification of optimization methodologies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to improve upon existing optimization algorithms by leveraging quantum mechanics, offering new insights and robust solutions to global optimization issues in areas like machine learning.

Method: The authors model the quantization process as a gradient-flow system, use a Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman (HJB) representation, reformulate the problem using the Schroedinger equation, and connect it to the Fokker-Planck equation.

Result: The framework enables quantum tunneling to escape local minima, provides thermodynamic interpretations of convergence, and unifies combinatorial and continuous optimization approaches. Numerical experiments show superior performance over traditional algorithms.

Conclusion: The analysis bridges quantum and thermodynamic optimization principles, revealing potent applications like image classification and enhancing optimization consistently across domains.

Abstract: This work presents a quantum mechanical framework for analyzing quantization-based optimization algorithms. The sampling process of the quantization-based search is modeled as a gradient-flow dissipative system, leading to a Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman (HJB) representation. Through a suitable transformation of the objective function, this formulation yields the Schroedinger equation, which reveals that quantum tunneling enables escape from local minima and guarantees access to the global optimum. By establishing the connection to the Fokker-Planck equation, the framework provides a thermodynamic interpretation of global convergence. Such an analysis between the thermodynamic and the quantum dynamic methodology unifies combinatorial and continuous optimization, and extends naturally to machine learning tasks such as image classification. Numerical experiments demonstrate that quantization-based optimization consistently outperforms conventional algorithms across both combinatorial problems and nonconvex continuous functions.

</details>


<div id='math.DS'></div>

# math.DS [[Back]](#toc)

### [468] [Exploring Collatz Dynamics with Human-LLM Collaboration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11066)
*Edward Y. Chang*

Main category: math.DS

TL;DR: The paper explores structural properties of the Collatz iteration by analyzing phenomena like modular scrambling and burst-gap trajectory decomposition, presenting novel structural lemmas and geometric distributions that suggest orbit contraction conditional on unproven hypotheses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To further understand structural dynamics of the Collatz iteration and propose an exploratory framework potentially explaining its convergence.

Method: Modular scrambling lemma, persistent exit lemma, and analysis of binary representation dynamics, along with statistical modeling of gaps and burst lengths using geometric distributions. Collaboration between humans and LLM is also documented.

Result: Key structural results include bijective behavior in modular scrambling, geometric distribution of gap lengths and valuation, persistent exit dynamics, and predictive orbit contraction under hypothetical assumptions.

Conclusion: A conditional exploratory framework is proposed for Collatz convergence. However, the main hypotheses for establishing convergence remain open, highlighting the need for further research.

Abstract: We investigate structural properties of the Collatz iteration through two phenomena observed in large computational exploration: modular scrambling of residue classes and a burst--gap decomposition of trajectories. We prove several structural results, including a modular scrambling lemma showing that the gap-return map acts as an exact bijection on high bits, a persistent exit lemma characterizing gap structure after persistent states, and a decay property for known portions of binary representations under gap-return dynamics. We further prove that, in the modular model, gap lengths and $2$-adic valuations follow geometric distributions, while persistent run lengths are geometric with expected burst length $E[B]=2$; together these predict strict orbit contraction. These results suggest a conditional framework in which convergence would follow from suitable orbitwise hypotheses on burst and gap lengths, which in turn are suggested by an orbit equidistribution conjecture. However, the key hypotheses remain open, and the framework is exploratory rather than a complete reduction. The paper also documents the human-LLM collaboration through which these observations were developed.

</details>


<div id='math.NA'></div>

# math.NA [[Back]](#toc)

### [469] [A Machine Learning-Enhanced Hopf-Cole Formulation for Nonlinear Gas Flow in Porous Media](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11250)
*V. S. Maduru,K. B. Nakshatrala*

Main category: math.NA

TL;DR: The paper introduces a novel framework for modeling gas flow in porous media which incorporates enhanced constitutive relations, transformed linear equations, neural networks, and advanced solvers for effective solutions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurately modeling gas flow through porous media is critical for applications like reservoir predictions and carbon capture but remains difficult due to nonlinearities and unknown parameters.

Method: A framework combining gas slippage effects with Klinkenberg-enhanced relations, Hopf-Cole transformation, shared-trunk neural networks, and a Deep Least-Squares solver was developed.

Result: The framework demonstrated robustness and efficiency in modeling flow dynamics and inverse modeling of hard-to-measure permeability and slippage parameters across varied pressure ranges.

Conclusion: The proposed methodology provides a powerful and efficient solution to simulate and analyze nonlinear gas transport in porous media, addressing key challenges in the field.

Abstract: Accurate modeling of gas flow through porous media is critical for many technological applications, including reservoir performance prediction, carbon capture and sequestration, and fuel cells and batteries. However, such modeling remains challenging due to strong nonlinear behavior and uncertainty in model parameters. In particular, gas slippage effects described by the Klinkenberg model introduce pressure-dependent permeability, which complicates numerical simulation and obscures deviations from classical Darcy flow behavior. To address these challenges, we present an integrated modeling framework for gas transport in porous media that combines a Klinkenberg-enhanced constitutive relation, Hopf-Cole-transformed mixed-form linear governing equations, a shared-trunk neural network architecture, and a Deep Least-Squares (DeepLS) solver. The Hopf-Cole transformation reformulates the original nonlinear flow equations into an equivalent linear system closely related to the Darcy model, while the mixed formulation, together with a shared-trunk neural architecture, enables simultaneous and accurate prediction of both pressure and velocity fields. A rigorous convergence analysis is performed both theoretically and numerically, establishing the stability and convergence properties of the proposed solver. Importantly, the proposed framework also naturally facilitates inverse modeling of pressure-dependent permeability and slippage parameters from limited or indirect observations, enabling efficient estimation of flow properties that are difficult to measure experimentally. Numerical results demonstrate accurate recovery of flow dynamics and parameters across a wide range of pressure regimes, highlighting the framework's robustness, accuracy, and computational efficiency for gas transport modeling and inversion in tight formations.

</details>


<div id='eess.IV'></div>

# eess.IV [[Back]](#toc)

### [470] [Deep Learning-based Assessment of the Relation Between the Third Molar and Mandibular Canal on Panoramic Radiographs using Local, Centralized, and Federated Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11850)
*Johan Andreas Balle Rubak,Sara Haghighat,Sanyam Jain,Mostafa Aldesoki,Akhilanand Chaurasia,Sarah Sadat Ehsani,Faezeh Dehghan Ghanatkaman,Ahmad Badruddin Ghazali,Julien Issa,Basel Khalil,Rishi Ramani,Ruben Pauwels*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: This study explored different machine learning paradigms—Local Learning (LL), Federated Learning (FL), and Centralized Learning (CL)—for classifying mandibular third molar-canal overlaps in panoramic radiographs, demonstrating CL as the most effective and FL a balanced, privacy-preserving solution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reducing the risk of inferior alveolar nerve injury by accurately classifying mandibular molar-canal overlap on panoramic radiographs, while utilizing federated learning to preserve patient privacy during multi-center collaboration.

Method: A pretrained ResNet-34 model was trained under Local Learning (LL), Federated Learning (FL), and Centralized Learning (CL) paradigms on cropped panoramic radiographs from eight independent labelers. Classification performance was assessed using AUC and other metrics supplemented with Grad-CAM analysis and training dynamics evaluation.

Result: Centralized Learning (CL) achieved the highest performance (AUC 0.831, accuracy = 0.782), Federated Learning (FL) showed intermediate results (AUC 0.757, accuracy = 0.703), while Local Learning (LL) generalized poorly across clients (mean AUC = 0.672).

Conclusion: Centralized Learning is the most effective approach for molar-canal classification, though Federated Learning serves as a promising privacy-preserving alternative with better performance than Local Learning.

Abstract: Impaction of the mandibular third molar in proximity to the mandibular canal increases the risk of inferior alveolar nerve injury. Panoramic radiography is routinely used to assess this relationship. Automated classification of molar-canal overlap could support clinical triage and reduce unnecessary CBCT referrals, while federated learning (FL) enables multi-center collaboration without sharing patient data. We compared Local Learning (LL), FL, and Centralized Learning (CL) for binary overlap/no-overlap classification on cropped panoramic radiographs partitioned across eight independent labelers. A pretrained ResNet-34 was trained under each paradigm and evaluated using per-client metrics with locally optimized thresholds and pooled test performance with a global threshold. Performance was assessed using area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC) and threshold-based metrics, alongside training dynamics, Grad-CAM visualizations, and server-side aggregate monitoring signals. On the test set, CL achieved the highest performance (AUC 0.831; accuracy = 0.782), FL showed intermediate performance (AUC 0.757; accuracy = 0.703), and LL generalized poorly across clients (AUC range = 0.619-0.734; mean = 0.672). Training curves suggested overfitting, particularly in LL models, and Grad-CAM indicated more anatomically focused attention in CL and FL. Overall, centralized training provided the strongest performance, while FL offers a privacy-preserving alternative that outperforms LL.

</details>


<div id='math.ST'></div>

# math.ST [[Back]](#toc)

### [471] [Conformal e-prediction in the presence of confounding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11134)
*Vladimir Vovk,Ruodu Wang*

Main category: math.ST

TL;DR: The paper extends conformal e-prediction by addressing observed confounding between $X$ and $Y$, focusing on IID settings and scenarios with dependent observations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable conformal e-prediction in scenarios where observed confounding exists between the random object $X$ and its label $Y$, and to account for settings with dependencies in data.

Method: The authors adapt conformal e-prediction methods for both IID data and data allowing dependencies between observations.

Result: The paper provides an extended framework for conformal e-prediction to manage both independent and dependent data with observed confounding.

Conclusion: Conformal e-prediction can accommodate observed confounding and dependencies, enhancing its applicability to varied data scenarios.

Abstract: This note extends conformal e-prediction to cover the case where there is observed confounding between the random object $X$ and its label $Y$. We consider both the case where the observed data is IID and a case where some dependence between observations is permitted.

</details>


<div id='q-fin.ST'></div>

# q-fin.ST [[Back]](#toc)

### [472] [Beyond Polarity: Multi-Dimensional LLM Sentiment Signals for WTI Crude Oil Futures Return Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11408)
*Dehao Dai,Ding Ma,Dou Liu,Kerui Geng,Yiqing Wang*

Main category: q-fin.ST

TL;DR: This paper explores improving crude oil price forecasting using multi-dimensional sentiment signals from large language models, finding that these signals enhance prediction accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional polarity-based sentiment measures fail to fully capture market-relevant information embedded in vast unstructured news data. This study seeks to improve forecasting of crude oil futures returns using multi-dimensional sentiment dimensions derived from large language models.

Method: The researchers utilized energy-sector news articles (2020–2025) to construct five sentiment dimensions—relevance, polarity, intensity, uncertainty, and forwardness—using GPT-4o, Llama 3.2-3b, and benchmarks like FinBERT and AlphaVantage. They aggregated the signals weekly and performed predictive evaluation in a classification framework.

Result: Combining GPT-4o and FinBERT models produced the best predictive results. SHAP analysis highlighted intensity and uncertainty as the most significant predictors, showcasing that sentiment metrics beyond simple polarity improve forecasting.

Conclusion: Multi-dimensional sentiment analysis incorporating large language models provides complementary and superior predictive information, supporting more accurate commodity return forecasts and energy market risk monitoring.

Abstract: Forecasting crude oil prices remains challenging because market-relevant information is embedded in large volumes of unstructured news and is not fully captured by traditional polarity-based sentiment measures. This paper examines whether multi-dimensional sentiment signals extracted by large language models improve the prediction of weekly WTI crude oil futures returns. Using energy-sector news articles from 2020 to 2025, we construct five sentiment dimensions covering relevance, polarity, intensity, uncertainty, and forwardness based on GPT-4o, Llama 3.2-3b, and two benchmark models, FinBERT and AlphaVantage. We aggregate article-level signals to the weekly level and evaluate their predictive performance in a classification framework. The best results are achieved by combining GPT-4o and FinBERT, suggesting that LLM-based and conventional financial sentiment models provide complementary predictive information. SHAP analysis further shows that intensity- and uncertainty-related features are among the most important predictors, indicating that the predictive value of news sentiment extends beyond simple polarity. Overall, the results suggest that multi-dimensional LLM-based sentiment measures can improve commodity return forecasting and support energy-market risk monitoring.

</details>


<div id='cs.CY'></div>

# cs.CY [[Back]](#toc)

### [473] [The Landscape of Generative AI in Information Systems: A Synthesis of Secondary Reviews and Research Agendas](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11842)
*Aleksander Jarzębowicz,Adam Przybyłek,Jacinto Estima,Yen Ying Ng,Jakub Swacha,Beata Zielosko,Lech Madeyski,Noel Carroll,Kai-Kristian Kemell,Bartosz Marcinkowski,Alberto Rodrigues da Silva,Viktoria Stray,Netta Iivari,Anh Nguyen-Duc,Jorge Melegati,Boris Delibašić,Emilio Insfran*

Main category: cs.CY

TL;DR: Analyzes 28 papers on Generative AI to highlight its transformative potential and challenges such as technical issues, ethical risks, and governance gaps. Proposes research agenda to address socio-technical misalignments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To synthesize current knowledge about Generative AI adoption and its challenges while proposing ways to bridge socio-technical gaps.

Method: Conducted systematic literature review of 28 relevant papers published since 2023, applying a socio-technical lens to interpret findings.

Result: Identifies numerous challenges like technical unreliability, societal-ethical risks, and governance gaps. Highlights misalignment between fast-evolving technical systems and slower social adaptations.

Conclusion: IS research should shift focus from analyzing GenAI impacts to actively shaping its evolution in alignment with organizational, societal, and regulatory needs.

Abstract: As organizations grapple with the rapid adoption of Generative AI (GenAI), this study synthesizes the state of knowledge through a systematic literature review of secondary studies and research agendas. Analyzing 28 papers published since 2023, we find that while GenAI offers transformative potential for productivity and innovation, its adoption is constrained by multiple interrelated challenges, including technical unreliability (hallucinations, performance drift), societal-ethical risks (bias, misuse, skill erosion), and a systemic governance vacuum (privacy, accountability, intellectual property). Interpreted through a socio-technical lens, these findings reveal a persistent misalignment between GenAI's fast-evolving technical subsystem and the slower-adapting social subsystem, positioning IS research as critical for achieving joint optimization. To bridge this gap, we discuss a research agenda that reorients IS scholarship from analyzing impacts toward actively shaping the co-evolution of technical capabilities with organizational procedures, societal values, and regulatory institutions--emphasizing hybrid human--AI ensembles, situated validation, design principles for probabilistic systems, and adaptive governance.

</details>


<div id='cs.SD'></div>

# cs.SD [[Back]](#toc)

### [474] [Uni-ASR: Unified LLM-Based Architecture for Non-Streaming and Streaming Automatic Speech Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11123)
*Yinfeng Xia,Jian Tang,Junfeng Hou,Gaopeng Xu,Haitao Yao*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: Uni-ASR integrates non-streaming and streaming speech recognition into one framework using LLMs, achieving high performance in both modes while addressing latency issues.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deploying ASR systems integrated with LLMs in low-latency streaming scenarios faces significant challenges, prompting the need for a unified framework that maintains performance while addressing latency constraints.

Method: Uni-ASR employs a unified framework leveraging joint training to transition between non-streaming and streaming modes effortlessly. Additionally, it uses context-aware training and a fallback decoding strategy to improve streaming accuracy without adding latency.

Result: Uni-ASR exhibits competitive results in non-streaming mode and demonstrates strong effectiveness in streaming scenarios under various latency constraints.

Conclusion: The proposed Uni-ASR framework is effective for both non-streaming and streaming speech recognition tasks, addressing latency issues and exhibiting high adaptability across modes.

Abstract: Although the deep integration of the Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) system with Large Language Models (LLMs) has significantly improved accuracy, the deployment of such systems in low-latency streaming scenarios remains challenging. In this paper, we propose Uni-ASR, a unified framework based on LLMs that integrates both non-streaming and streaming speech recognition capabilities. We propose a joint training paradigm that enables the system to seamlessly transition between two recognition modes without any architectural modifications. Furthermore, we introduce a context-aware training paradigm and a co-designed fallback decoding strategy, which can enhance streaming recognition accuracy without introducing additional latency. The experimental results demonstrate that Uni-ASR not only achieves competitive performance within non-streaming mode, but also demonstrates strong effectiveness in streaming scenarios under diverse latency constraints.

</details>


### [475] [AnimeScore: A Preference-Based Dataset and Framework for Evaluating Anime-Like Speech Style](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11482)
*Joonyong Park,Jerry Li*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: AnimeScore provides an objective framework to evaluate 'anime-like' voices using pairwise rankings, addressing the lack of reliable metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is no standardized objective metric for evaluating 'anime-like' voices, which are currently judged through costly subjective methods. The study aims to overcome the unreliability of traditional MOS protocols for this purpose.

Method: The authors developed AnimeScore, a preference-based evaluation framework, collecting 15,000 pairwise judgments from 187 evaluators. Acoustic analysis and SSL-based ranking models were used to evaluate anime-like voice metrics.

Result: The researchers found that handcrafted acoustic features reached a 69.3% AUC ceiling, while SSL-based ranking models achieved a significantly higher AUC score of up to 90.8%, validating the practicality of the proposed framework.

Conclusion: AnimeScore provides an effective and data-driven method for quantifying anime-likeness in voices, delivering insights into acoustic preferences and aiding the optimization of generative speech models.

Abstract: Evaluating 'anime-like' voices currently relies on costly subjective judgments, yet no standardized objective metric exists. A key challenge is that anime-likeness, unlike naturalness, lacks a shared absolute scale, making conventional Mean Opinion Score (MOS) protocols unreliable. To address this gap, we propose AnimeScore, a preference-based framework for automatic anime-likeness evaluation via pairwise ranking. We collect 15,000 pairwise judgments from 187 evaluators with free-form descriptions, and acoustic analysis reveals that perceived anime-likeness is driven by controlled resonance shaping, prosodic continuity, and deliberate articulation rather than simple heuristics such as high pitch. We show that handcrafted acoustic features reach a 69.3% AUC ceiling, while SSL-based ranking models achieve up to 90.8% AUC, providing a practical metric that can also serve as a reward signal for preference-based optimization of generative speech models.

</details>


### [476] [Resurfacing Paralinguistic Awareness in Large Audio Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11947)
*Hao Yang,Minghan Wang,Tongtong Wu,Lizhen Qu,Ehsan Shareghi,Gholamreza Haffari*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: This paper addresses the issue of large audio language models (LALMs) neglecting paralinguistic (non-content) cues in speech by proposing a fine-tuning protocol that improves their paralinguistic awareness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LALMs currently focus exclusively on content within speech interactions, neglecting paralinguistic cues that provide extra user context and improve human interaction. This gap limits their potential in speech-based applications.

Method: The authors conduct five layer-wise analyses to identify model layers for understanding both semantics and paralinguistics. They then develop a paralinguistic-enhanced fine-tuning (PE-FT) protocol, which includes selective-layer fine-tuning and an auxiliary dual-level classification head.

Result: The proposed PE-FT protocol effectively resurfaces paralinguistic capabilities in LALMs and outperforms traditional all-layer fine-tuning strategies.

Conclusion: Equipping LALMs with paralinguistic awareness through the PE-FT protocol enhances their ability to process speech interactions, showing efficiency and improvement in performance.

Abstract: Large Audio Language Models (LALMs) have expanded the interaction with human to speech modality, which introduces great interactive potential, due to the paralinguistic cues implicitly indicating the user context. However, building on the current content-centred paradigm, LALMs usually neglect such paralinguistic cues and respond solely based on query content. In this work, to resurface the paralinguistic awareness in LALMs, we introduce five diverse layer-wise analyses to jointly identify paralinguistic layers and semantic understanding layers. Based on these insights, we propose a paralinguistic-enhanced fine-tuning (PE-FT) protocol accordingly to equip LALMs with paralinguistic-aware capabilities, including (1) selective-layer fine-tuning, and (2) an auxiliary dual-level classification head. Our experiments demonstrate that PE-FT protocol efficiently and effectively resurfaces the paralinguistic awareness, even surpassing the performance of the all-layer fine-tuning strategy.

</details>


### [477] [Toward Complex-Valued Neural Networks for Waveform Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11589)
*Hyung-Seok Oh,Deok-Hyeon Cho,Seung-Bin Kim,Seong-Whan Lee*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: ComVo is a complex-valued neural vocoder that enhances waveform synthesis by integrating native complex arithmetic and phase quantization, achieving higher quality and more efficient training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitations of current real-valued iSTFT neural vocoders in capturing the inherent structure of complex spectrograms and to optimize training efficiency.

Method: ComVo employs a generator and discriminator with native complex arithmetic for adversarial training, introduces phase quantization to guide phase transformations, and uses a block-matrix computation scheme to improve training efficiency.

Result: ComVo outperforms real-valued baselines in audio synthesis quality and reduces training time by 25%.

Conclusion: The proposed ComVo framework demonstrates improved efficiency and synthesis quality over existing vocoders, proving its potential for advancing neural waveform generation techniques.

Abstract: Neural vocoders have recently advanced waveform generation, yielding natural and expressive audio. Among these approaches, iSTFT-based vocoders have recently gained attention. They predict a complex-valued spectrogram and then synthesize the waveform via iSTFT, thereby avoiding learned upsampling stages that can increase computational cost. However, current approaches use real-valued networks that process the real and imaginary parts independently. This separation limits their ability to capture the inherent structure of complex spectrograms. We present ComVo, a Complex-valued neural Vocoder whose generator and discriminator use native complex arithmetic. This enables an adversarial training framework that provides structured feedback in complex-valued representations. To guide phase transformations in a structured manner, we introduce phase quantization, which discretizes phase values and regularizes the training process. Finally, we propose a block-matrix computation scheme to improve training efficiency by reducing redundant operations. Experiments demonstrate that ComVo achieves higher synthesis quality than comparable real-valued baselines, and that its block-matrix scheme reduces training time by 25%. Audio samples and code are available at https://hs-oh-prml.github.io/ComVo/.

</details>


### [478] [Continued Pretraining for Low-Resource Swahili ASR: Achieving State-of-the-Art Performance with Minimal Labeled Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11378)
*Hillary Mutisya,John Mugane*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: The research explores improving Swahili ASR using CPT with wav2vec2-bert-2.0, yielding a significant performance enhancement and a replicable model for low-resource languages.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenges in automatic speech recognition (ASR) for low-resource languages like Swahili using limited labeled data.

Method: The study employs continued pretraining (CPT) on unlabeled audio, coupled with pseudo-labeling and supervised finetuning on 20,000 labeled samples.

Result: Achieved a 3.24% word error rate (WER) on Common Voice Swahili, an 82% improvement over the baseline and 61% better than the previous best academic system (8.3% WER with XLS-R).

Conclusion: The approach demonstrates the effectiveness of CPT for low-resource languages, providing a scalable and replicable method for better ASR systems in such contexts.

Abstract: We investigate continued pretraining (CPT) for adapting wav2vec2-bert-2.0 to Swahili automatic speech recognition (ASR). Our approach combines unlabeled audio with limited labeled data through pseudo-labeled CPT followed by supervised finetuning. With 20,000 labeled samples, we achieve 3.24% WER on Common Voice Swahili-an 82% relative improvement over the baseline. This result surpasses the best previously reported academic system (8.3% WER from XLS-R) by 61% relative improvement. We provide concrete data requirements and a replicable methodology applicable to other low-resource languages.

</details>


### [479] [Causal Prosody Mediation for Text-to-Speech:Counterfactual Training of Duration, Pitch, and Energy in FastSpeech2](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11683)
*Suvendu Sekhar Mohanty*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: The paper presents a novel causal framework in expressive text-to-speech (TTS), integrating emotional prosody control into FastSpeech2 for better naturalness and accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance expressive TTS by disentangling emotional prosody from linguistic content while improving controllability and interpretability of prosody.

Method: The study applies a causal model within the FastSpeech2 architecture using emotion conditioning and counterfactual objectives. It incorporates two loss functions, IPC and CPC, for emotion-prosody alignment and trains on multi-speaker emotional speech corpora.

Result: Achieves superior performance in emotion rendering, prosody manipulation, and cross-speaker emotion transfer with notable improvements in MOS, emotion accuracy, intelligibility, and speaker consistency.

Conclusion: The model successfully enables controlled prosody editing and enhances the interpretability of TTS systems. Limitations include assumptions about prosody parameters, but the method advances expressive and controllable speech synthesis through causal learning.

Abstract: We propose a novel causal prosody mediation framework for expressive text-to-speech (TTS) synthesis. Our approach augments the FastSpeech2 architecture with explicit emotion conditioning and introduces counterfactual training objectives to disentangle emotional prosody from linguistic content. By formulating a structural causal model of how text (content), emotion, and speaker jointly influence prosody (duration, pitch, energy) and ultimately the speech waveform, we derive two complementary loss terms: an Indirect Path Constraint (IPC) to enforce that emotion affects speech only through prosody, and a Counterfactual Prosody Constraint (CPC) to encourage distinct prosody patterns for different emotions. The resulting model is trained on multi-speaker emotional corpora (LibriTTS, EmoV-DB, VCTK) with a combined objective that includes standard spectrogram reconstruction and variance prediction losses alongside our causal losses. In evaluations on expressive speech synthesis, our method achieves significantly improved prosody manipulation and emotion rendering, with higher mean opinion scores (MOS) and emotion accuracy than baseline FastSpeech2 variants. We also observe better intelligibility (low WER) and speaker consistency when transferring emotions across speakers. Extensive ablations confirm that the causal objectives successfully separate prosody attribution, yielding an interpretable model that allows controlled counterfactual prosody editing (e.g. "same utterance, different emotion") without compromising naturalness. We discuss the implications for identifiability in prosody modeling and outline limitations such as the assumption that emotion effects are fully captured by pitch, duration, and energy. Our work demonstrates how integrating causal learning principles into TTS can improve controllability and expressiveness in generated speech.

</details>


<div id='cs.CC'></div>

# cs.CC [[Back]](#toc)

### [480] [On the Computational Hardness of Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.11332)
*Barna Saha,Yinzhan Xu,Christopher Ye,Hantao Yu*

Main category: cs.CC

TL;DR: The paper provides the first non-trivial computational lower bounds for multi-head, multi-layer transformers, demonstrating that separate computation of attention heads is essentially optimal under SETH and bounding the arithmetic operations for small and large embedding regimes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether transformers can be computed more efficiently than independently evaluating each attention layer and head, building upon questions in computational complexity like direct sum problems.

Method: Used theoretical analysis, leveraging concepts such as SETH and the Baur-Strassen theorem, to derive computational lower bounds for the transformer attention mechanism in both small and large embedding regimes.
